description:
Close the @spec/@type epic with two things: REPL surfacing of declared specs alongside inferred, and a golden test asserting consistent verdicts across interpreter/JIT/AOT.

## REPL surfacing

Extend commit a166324's `?<name>` REPL command (src/repl.rs) to show, when present, the declared @spec alongside what the typer inferred. Layout:

```
?add1
fn add1/1
  @spec   integer -> integer    (declared)
          int_lit_41 -> int_lit_42  (inferred at fixtures/add1.fz:7)
  @doc    "Add one to n"
```

When no @spec is declared, only show inferred (current behavior).

## Three-path parity golden test

Add a fixture (fixtures/spec_violation.fz) with a deliberately wrong @spec:

```
@spec add1(float) :: float
fn add1(n) do n + 1 end

fn main() do
  print(add1(41))   # int callsite — violates declared float
end
```

Test harness asserts:
- `fz run` (interpreter path): errors with the same @spec violation diagnostic
- `fz jit` (JIT path): errors with the same diagnostic
- `fz build` (AOT path): errors with the same diagnostic
- All three error messages are byte-identical (or differ only in path prefixes)

Add a positive companion fixture (fixtures/spec_ok.fz) where the declared spec matches and all three paths produce identical output.

## Why this matters

Three-path parity is a project invariant (CLAUDE.md). The validation pass (.31.5) runs in the shared compile_with_backend path used by JIT and AOT; the interpreter has its own driver. This ticket *proves* parity rather than asserting it.

## Out of scope

- IDE integration / LSP surfacing — separate concern
- Pretty-printing of complex Descrs — falls under .20.8
title: REPL ?<name> @spec surfacing + three-path parity test
